sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The RACE scale is a prehospital assessment tool used in patients with suspected stroke to help identify possible large vessel occlusion (LVO). It is an adjunct to, not a replacement for, standard stroke recognition and urgent transport pathways. The scale assesses facial palsy, arm motor impairment, leg motor impairment, head and gaze deviation, and aphasia or agnosia.

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Use RACE only after stroke has already been suspected clinically
- RACE should not delay last-known-well determination, stroke pre-notification, or transport to an appropriate stroke-capable centre
- A low or negative RACE score does not exclude stroke, posterior circulation stroke, or the need for urgent transport
- Apply the tool consistently and document the score alongside the wider neurological examination

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Confirm suspected stroke using FAST or BE-FAST and document the exact last-known-well time
- Use RACE as an adjunct when the patient is clinically suspected of having stroke and the score may assist destination or pre-notification decisions
- Pre-notify the receiving service and communicate both the clinical stroke findings and the RACE score
- Do not let completion of the tool delay airway support, blood glucose assessment, or rapid transport

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Integrate the RACE score with the full stroke assessment, including conscious state, glucose, ECG where appropriate, and transport time
- Use the score to support discussions with receiving stroke services, especially when large vessel occlusion is suspected
- Continue to manage the patient under the stroke and TIA guideline rather than as a stand-alone pathway
